query: PAS positive granules in infant liver biopsy

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Jaundice -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: A liver biopsy is usually necessary for a definitive diagnosis of cholestasis as it helps to differentiate biliary atresia from idiopathic neonatal hepatitis. Histopathologic features of BA include the expansion of the hepatic portal tracts with edema, fibrodysplasia, bile ductular proliferation, and the presence of bile plugs in the ductal lumen. Multinucleate giant cells and hemopoiesis are also findings noted on histopathologic examinations of cholestatic liver samples. [85] Although not diagnostic of any particular disorder, the prominence of hepatic erythropoiesis is seen more frequently in cholestasis of infectious etiology. Pathognomonic histologic findings of other cholestatic conditions include periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) positive granules noted with alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, a paucity of bile ducts associated with Alagille syndrome, and periductal necrosis and inflammation seen in sclerosing cholangitis. [86] Among familial causes of cholestasis, canalicular cholestasis with a marked absence of ductular proliferation and isolated periportal biliary metaplasia of hepatocytes is commonly seen in patients with PFIC type 1. Though the histopathology of PFIC type 2 is similar, the altered liver architecture and extensive lobular and portal fibrosis with inflammation occur more frequently. [53]

retrieved_docs.title: Glycogen Storage Disease Type I -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Although genetic testing has largely replaced the need for liver biopsy, liver histology in GSD I shows hepatocytes filled with periodic acid-Schiff (PAS)-positive and diastase-sensitive glycogen. [9] In some cases, liver biopsy may be performed in patients with unexplained hepatomegaly or when genetic testing is inconclusive. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Glycogenic Hepatopathy -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Liver biopsy is the gold standard for diagnosis of glycogenic hepatopathy, typical features are swollen hepatocytes, due to the accumulation of glycogen in the cytoplasm. The hematoxylin and eosin (HE) stain shows pale and enlarged hepatocytes with numerous glycogenated nuclei. Diastase enzyme, when added to the periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) stained specimen, causes enzymatic degradation of glycogen, causing empty hepatocytes (“ghost cells”). [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Glycogen Storage Disease -- Evaluation -- Biopsy
retrieved_docs.content: Muscle biopsies typically reveal diastase-sensitive vacuoles that yield a positive staining result with periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) and acid phosphatase in GSD type IV. [17] In GSD type V, a muscle biopsy should demonstrate negative histochemical staining for phosphorylase activity. In the absence of phosphorylase activity, the specimen remains brown instead of taking up a deep blue hue. In addition, the biopsy should reveal subsarcolemmal deposits of glycogen detected by PAS staining. [18] In patients with GSD type XV, muscle biopsies often show PAS-positive inclusions that are not digested with α-amylase treatment and, on electron microscopy, may be seen as filamentous material corresponding to polyglucosan bodies. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Hepatic Porphyria -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: This can show subepidermal vesicles and marked thickening of superficial dermal vessels. There is minimal or no inflammation. In porphyria cutanea tarda, dermal papillae protrude into bulla with a festooned pattern. The roof of the blisters has eosinophilic, PAS+, and diastase-resistant linear globules.

retrieved_docs.title: Gilbert Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: A liver biopsy is generally not required for patients suspected of having Gilbert syndrome unless other confounding diagnoses merit consideration. A liver biopsy, if done, will show normal histology. Nonspecific lipofuscin pigment found within the centrilobular region of the biopsy has also been described. [25] [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Hereditary Hemochromatosis -- Diagnostic Tests -- Testing for Liver Involvement
retrieved_docs.content: Histopathological evaluation of the liver in HH reveals characteristic intracytoplasmic deposits of golden-yellow hemosiderin granules within hepatocytes. These deposits are initially located in the periportal region (see Image. Micrograph of Hemochromatosis) but eventually are found throughout the lobule, including within Kupffer cells and biliary epithelium. These hemosiderin granules will appear blue with the Prussian blue stain (Image 3 - Hemochromatosis; Prussian Blue Stain). Parenchymal architecture is preserved in early-stage HH; the onset of cirrhosis distorts normal architecture.

retrieved_docs.title: Glycogen Storage Disease -- Evaluation -- Biopsy
retrieved_docs.content: Although specific genetic testing is now available for diagnosing most GSDs, histologic examination of liver or muscle biopsy is still used in specific scenarios. In GSD type 0, a liver biopsy typically shows decreased hepatic glycogen and can make a definitive diagnosis for this disease. [15] In GSD type I, a liver biopsy should reveal pale-staining, swollen hepatocytes, steatosis, and nuclear hyper-glycogenation. Fibrosis is another common finding on liver biopsy in patients with GSDs and is predominant in patients with GSD type III, IV, and VI. In GSD type III, periportal fibrosis and micronodular cirrhosis are often seen with distended hepatocytes due to excess glycogen accumulation. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Budd-Chiari Syndrome -- Evaluation -- Liver Biopsy
retrieved_docs.content: Liver biopsy may be diagnostic, often showing necrosis, hemorrhage, and congestion in the central zone.

retrieved_docs.title: Glycogenic Hepatopathy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Glycogenic hepatopathy (GH) is a rare or possibly under-diagnosed complication seen in children and young adults with poorly controlled type 1 diabetes mellitus and few patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus. [1] [2] It is characterized by a reversible accumulation of excess glycogen in hepatocytes, causing hepatomegaly and transient elevation of liver enzymes, especially transaminases. The diagnosis is confirmed by liver biopsy and staining of glycogen using hematoxylin and eosin (HE) stain.

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Atresia -- Evaluation -- Liver Biopsy
retrieved_docs.content: Liver biopsy can differentiate biliary atresia from other causes of cholestatic jaundice with a high level of accuracy. Features suggestive of biliary atresia include bile duct proliferation, bile plugging, multinucleated giant cells, focal necrosis of liver parenchyma, extramedullary hemopoiesis, and inflammatory cell infiltrate. Among these features, bile ductular proliferation is considered the most sensitive and specific feature. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Dubin-Johnson Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The liver is grossly black in appearance and on light microscopy; the architecture of the liver is normal, but there is the accumulation of dark, coarsely granular pigment in the centrilobular hepatocytes; this feature is not seen in Rotor syndrome. On electron microscopy, the pigment appears to be within the lysosomes. Based upon the pigment's histochemical staining and physicochemical properties, it appears that it is related to melanin. [2] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatopathology Evaluation of Metabolic and Storage Diseases -- Clinical Significance -- Lysosomal Storage Disorders
retrieved_docs.content: Niemann-Pick disease is an autosomal recessive disease caused by mutations in the genes encoding the lysosomal enzyme sphingomyelinase, resulting in the accumulation of sphingomyelin and other lipid byproducts within tissues. [54] [55] [56] Over the past several decades, numerous genes and mutations have been implicated in the pathogenesis of Niemann-Pick disease. [54] [55] [56] Clinically, all patients experience progressive neurologic deterioration and eventual death during early childhood. [54] [57] Additional clinical features are mutation-dependent, including hepatosplenomegaly, interstitial lung disease, thrombocytopenia, and ocular disturbances. [58] Cutaneous lesions are not classic but can include brown hyperpigmentation and brown indurated plaques, [59] facial papules, [60] juvenile xanthogranulomas, [61] erythematous and yellow papules, and deep-seated subcutaneous nodules. [62] On light microscopy, the brown plaques displayed dermal foamy histiocytes with scattered lymphocytes and plasma cells, similar to the appearance in other organs. [59] In a report, these foamy cells appeared brown on hematoxylin and eosin staining. [60] Deep-seated nodules revealed lobular panniculitis composed of a mixed inflammatory infiltrate and scattered ill-defined epithelioid granulomas. [62] Foamy cells stain with oil red O and Sudan black on frozen sections and appear metachromatic with toluidine blue. [60] [63]

retrieved_docs.title: Autoimmune Hepatitis and Pregnancy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: For individuals with consistent clinical or laboratory features, it is recommended to obtain serum anti-nuclear (ANA), anti-smooth muscle (ASMA), anti-mitochondrial (AMA), and anti-liver/kidney microsomal-1 (ALKM-1) antibodies, as well as either an immunoglobulin G (IgG) or gamma globulin level. It is recommended for those who are negative for these autoantibodies to obtain anti-soluble liver antigen/liver pancreas antibody, anti-actin antibodies, and atypical perinuclear antineutrophil cytoplasmic (p-ANCA) antibodies. Patients with AIH historically categorize into two disease subtypes according to the nature of autoantibodies [2] : Type 1 AIH: Anti-nuclear (ANA) and/or anti-smooth muscle (ASMA) antibodies with F-actin specificity Type 2 AIH: Anti-liver/kidney microsomal antibody type 1 (ALKM-1) and/or anti-liver cytosol antibody type 1 (ALC-1)

retrieved_docs.title: Gaucher Disease -- Evaluation -- Liver Biopsy
retrieved_docs.content: A liver biopsy may be performed to evaluate unexplained hepatomegaly. However, a biopsy is rarely necessary because a specific diagnostic test is available.

retrieved_docs.title: Mallory Bodies -- Morphology
retrieved_docs.content: Although hematoxylin-eosin staining can reveal MDBs, immunohistochemical staining for cytokeratin or ubiquitin offers greater sensitivity and diagnostic specificity. Hepatocytes containing MDBs are often surrounded by pericellular fibrosis and infiltrating neutrophils, a phenomenon known as satellitosis. [20] The intracellular distribution of MDBs reflects their stage of development. Early-stage MDBs appear as small cytoplasmic globules. Mature forms present as large paranuclear inclusions. Older forms are typically found at the cell periphery. The zonal distribution of MDBs varies by disease. In PBC and Wilson disease, MDBs localize to zone 1 of the hepatic lobule, whereas in ASH and MASLD, they are predominantly found in zone 3.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1638
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic ultrasound of the gastrointestinal tract is an imaging modality employed to evaluate the abdominal organs and structures. Within this broader category, the liver ultrasound (فحص الكبد بالموجات فوق الصوتية) is recognized as a specific child examination that focuses on assessing hepatic anatomy, detecting liver lesions, and monitoring hepatic disease. This hierarchical relationship underscores that the liver ultrasound is a specialized component of the overall gastrointestinal tract diagnostic ultrasound, contributing to a comprehensive abdominal assessment in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 210
retrieved_docs.content: modalities, procedural interventions, and disease processes are anatomically and clinically related, providing a comprehensive framework for the evaluation and management of abdominal, hepatic, and biliary disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1081
retrieved_docs.content: NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN is a complex neonatal condition that gives rise to several related complications. One important manifestation is neonatal jaundice that results from delayed conjugation due to the immature development of the hepatic conjugating system. In addition, the condition includes نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage), which reflects the hemorrhagic component of the disease. Various forms of neonatal jaundice are also linked to this disorder: يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice) in general, يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (neonatal jaundice caused by hemolysis of the fetus or newborn), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (jaundice associated with preterm birth). Together, these interrelated entities illustrate how hemorrhagic and hemolytic processes in the newborn can impair liver function, lead to delayed bilirubin conjugation, and produce respiratory bleeding, underscoring the need for comprehensive assessment and coordinated management of affected infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 585
retrieved_docs.content: Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings encompass a broad spectrum of atypical laboratory results, and within this category a positive culture (المزرعة إيجابية) is identified as a specific child finding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 237
retrieved_docs.content: positivity intersect with these gastrointestinal pathologies. Recognizing these interconnections supports comprehensive assessment, diagnosis, and management of patients presenting with overlapping gastrointestinal and hematologic findings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1199
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is identified as a neonatal condition, indicating that it manifests during the newborn period. The neonatal qualifier encompasses several distinct clinical entities. Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system represents a form of early‑life hyperbilirubinemia caused by the immature hepatic capacity to process bilirubin. In addition, neonatal hemorrhage after birth (نزف سري بعد الولادة) and neonatal respiratory tract bleeding (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ) are recognized as bleeding complications that can occur in the immediate post‑natal period, affecting either the umbilical region or the respiratory passages. Another neonatal manifestation is nuclear jaundice resulting from the protective coating (التمنيع الإسوي) in embryos or newborns (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد), which reflects a specific type of jaundice linked to fetal or neonatal physiological factors. Together, these entities illustrate the range of disorders and complications that are characteristic of the neonatal stage, emphasizing the importance of early recognition and management of metabolic, hemorrhagic, and respiratory conditions in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 184
retrieved_docs.content: and hepatocellular carcinoma. Hepatocellular carcinoma represents a manifestation of cancer of the liver and intra‑hepatic bile ducts, and it is further related to cholestatic liver disease (مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ), illustrating a spectrum from malignant to non‑malignant hepatic disorders. Diagnostic and therapeutic procedures targeting the liver include liver biopsy (خزعة الكبد), performed on partially excised hepatic tissue, and ultrasound examination of the liver (فحص الكبد بالموجات فوق الصوتية), which is a child procedure of diagnostic ultrasound of the gastrointestinal tract. The liver also serves as an indirect site for various non‑gastrointestinal therapeutic interventions. Collectively, these interrelated concepts demonstrate the anatomical and functional integration of the gallbladder, liver, and biliary system; the utility of ultrasonographic techniques for biliary and hepatic pathology; the role of substances as causative agents for renal and hepatic toxicities; and the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 937
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is classified as a specific form of NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN. The broader category of NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN encompasses several distinct types of neonatal jaundice, including Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, the general condition يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice), يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (neonatal jaundice caused by hemolysis in the fetus or newborn), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (neonatal jaundice associated with preterm birth). These interrelated entities reflect different pathophysiological mechanisms—such as hemolysis, immature hepatic conjugation capacity, and prematurity—that can contribute to hemorrhagic and hemolytic disease in the newborn. Recognizing how Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome fits within this spectrum and how the various forms of neonatal jaundice relate to the overarching disease category is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and targeted management of affected infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1138
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is classified as a neonatal condition, indicating that it manifests during the newborn period. The neonatal (qualifier value) stage is also the context in which several specific clinical entities occur, including نزف سري بعد الولادة (umbilical bleeding after birth), نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract bleeding), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (premature‑related neonatal jaundice). Recognizing that these entities all occur in the neonatal timeframe helps clinicians understand the spectrum of disorders and presentations that may be present in newborns, including those affected by Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome, and guides appropriate evaluation and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2429
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn is a complex neonatal condition that can give rise to several related complications. One important manifestation is neonatal jaundice caused by delayed conjugation due to the immature development of the hepatic conjugating system. The disorder may also be accompanied by post‑natal umbilical bleeding (نزف سري بعد الولادة) and bleeding from the neonatal respiratory tract (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ). In addition, the disease can produce a form of fetal‑origin jaundice (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد) and a jaundice that is specifically linked to premature birth (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان). Recognizing these child conditions is essential for comprehensive assessment and management of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2676
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the liver and intrahepatic bile duct manifests as سرطان الخلايا الكبدية (liver cell carcinoma), which is broadly related to مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ (jaundiced liver disease). This connection indicates that hepatocellular carcinoma often presents within the spectrum of liver diseases that cause jaundice, highlighting the importance of recognizing jaundice as a potential clinical clue to underlying malignant liver pathology. Understanding this relationship aids clinicians in early detection, accurate diagnosis, and appropriate management of patients with liver‑related malignancies and associated jaundice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 1163
retrieved_docs.content: The qualifier “Neonatal (qualifier value)” designates conditions that manifest during the newborn period. It is applied to the occurrence of نزف سري بعد الولادة (umbilical bleeding after birth), نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract bleeding), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), يرقان نووي (physiologic neonatal jaundice), and يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (physiologic jaundice resulting from immature hepatic conjugation in fetuses or newborns). Recognizing these entities as neonatal helps clinicians focus assessment and management on disorders specific to the early post‑natal timeframe.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2682
retrieved_docs.content: Biopsy of liver is the term that classifies the Arabic phrase خزعة الكبد, indicating that the English medical procedure name corresponds directly to the Arabic term for a liver biopsy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 213
retrieved_docs.content: cirrhosis (تشَمُّع كَبِدي) and is associated with esophageal variceal bleeding. The knowledge base therefore integrates congenital correction, diagnostic imaging, endoscopic therapeutic techniques, variceal pathophysiology, and related hepatic risk factors, emphasizing the interconnected role of vascular morphology, procedural categories, and clinical associations in the management of esophageal and related gastrointestinal diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: Failure (biologic function) is associated with الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ, indicating that a general loss of biological function can be linked to hepatorenal failure—a severe condition in which both liver and kidney functions are compromised. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as it highlights the need to assess and manage systemic organ failure comprehensively in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 917
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is classified under neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn. This broader category encompasses several specific neonatal conditions, including نزف سري بعد الولادة (umbilical hemorrhage after birth), نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (respiratory tract hemorrhage in the newborn), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), and يرقان نووي (physiologic neonatal jaundice). Together, these entities represent key hemorrhagic and hyperbilirubinemic presentations that are important for the diagnosis, monitoring, and management of affected newborns.
